mnemonics.title: Recursive vs Recursively Enumerable
mnemonics.content: R.E.C.U.R.S.I.V.E. = Recursive: Enumeration terminates, Computation always halts; R.E. = Recursive Enumerable: Enumeration may not terminate, may loop forever on rejection

acronyms.usage: Languages accepted by TM that halts on acceptance but may loop on rejection
A TM that can simulate any other TM given its encoding
Set of all encodings ⟨M,w⟩ where TM M accepts w
Problems to which all NP problems reduce; not directly related to computability

visualTips.visualization: Draw three concentric circles: innermost = Regular, middle = Context-Free, outer = Recursive, largest = Recursively Enumerable. Show recursive inside r.e.
Draw a flowchart: Input → TM → (Accept if in L) OR (Reject if not in L) OR (Loop if r.e. and not in L). Show halt boxes for recursive, looping for r.e. only.
Draw: Problem A → Transform (reduction function f) → Problem B → Solution. Show: Solving B solves A through f.

analogies.analogy: Like a teacher who always gives a definite answer (pass/fail) to every student within fixed time - always terminates with decision.
Like a judge who announces 'guilty' immediately but says nothing (indefinitely) for 'not guilty' - may never give verdict for rejection.
Like trying to predict the future - no algorithm can look at any program and predict its behavior with certainty for all cases.
Like the 'is it art?' question - any non-trivial property about what's produced is undecidable because you'd need to solve the halting problem first.
